best_example.rep_response: Proactively offered NDIS pathway before objection fully formed
best_example.note: Best example of pre-handling — solved before it became a blocker. Leanne Black confirmed Deposit Paid.

worst_example.rep_response: "Off the top of my head, about $450, I think." Immediately offered to send email info without any objection handling.
worst_example.note: Worst price handling on record. Rep did not know his own payment plans, calculated live, retreated to email at first resistance. Bianca said 'I'll find a way' — a clear buying signal that Rye acknowledged with 'yeah yeah 100%' and booked a follow-up. Outcome confirmed: no close.

price_affordability.weekly_note: Apr 10: Jen Howgate (Bailey) — Mark had price shock at $2,700. Bailey offered alternatives (Lighthouse, Mentor Connect) but didn't isolate or attempt $111 with Mark directly. Bailey price objection count now 3. Mark was on the call — this is the both-parents pattern where the partner's price concern could have been addressed directly.

best_example.rep_response: Excellent isolation: 'If dad wasn't on board, what would happen?' — isolated the conditional and addressed the auth question cleanly. Achieved 10/10 after.
best_example.note: Best Anthony objection handling on record — proactive isolation + conditional close. Judith confirmed Active Sessions with W1 on Mar 29. Gold standard partner objection handling leads to confirmed activation.

worst_example.parent_quote: Let me chat to Jason and myself.
worst_example.note: CRITICAL miss: Jason had been on the call the entire time. Elizabeth cited him as the decision-maker. Tom accepted the deferral without turning to Jason directly. The sequence: turn to dad — 'Jason, you've heard everything — does this feel like what Michael needs?' — was not used. Outcome hardened to Call Objection within 2 days. Both of this week's Call Objection outcomes trace to this same pattern.

partner_coparent.weekly_note: Apr 10: Jen Howgate (Bailey) — Jen deferred to Mark about finances, but Mark was ON THE CALL. Bailey never turned to Mark directly. This is the Elisabeth Poole/Kirian Andreou pattern now appearing in Bailey's calls too. Partner-on-call close miss is becoming a team-wide pattern.

best_example.parent_quote: He'll be anti it to start with. It's all about picking your moment.
best_example.rep_response: Dentist metaphor delivered naturally: 'It's a bit like getting them to the dentist, just get them in the chair and they'll get it done. You get them on the phone with us and boys that were reluctant will be buzzing for the next call.'
best_example.note: Mar 29 CONFIRMED: Bec Richardson = Deposit Paid. Tom's dentist metaphor + pre-emptive son buy-in handling contributed to a clean close. Grade 5 from confirmed close only.

son_buyin.weekly_note: Apr 2 update: Tom's dentist metaphor now deployed on all 4 calls naturally. Pre-emptive handling is gold standard. New data point: Lorissa/Samuel (organised resistance), James/Tommy (flat no), Emily/Charlie (gaming distraction), Sky/Sam (guarded). All handled at 4/5 with dentist.

best_example.parent_quote: Want to wait until Koban gets back from Bendigo
best_example.rep_response: Used son's return as urgency window
best_example.note: Decent urgency reframe but financial blocker not addressed

best_example.parent_quote: I've heard all this before. And I'm pretty sure none of this is going to help.
best_example.rep_response: Acknowledged skepticism, stayed warm, continued with pitch.
best_example.note: Beck Reside confirmed Follow Up. The 'past failure' + financial constraint combination was likely unwinnable regardless. Anthony read the room correctly.

best_example.parent_quote: It all sounds very good. Like I said, I've heard all this before.
best_example.rep_response: Maintained warmth but didn't use the gold standard 'what missed the mark' question to reframe
best_example.note: Gold standard from Harry: 'What do you think missed the mark with previous supports?' Not used here. Outcome: Follow Up — likely unwinnable given financial constraints, but the gold standard question should still be in every call.

best_example.parent_quote: I need to have a chat with my husband first
best_example.rep_response: Partial isolation — 'if Anthony said no, would you still go?' secured a YES.
best_example.note: Got the conditional YES. Failed to convert it to a close.

worst_example.rep_response: A hundred percent. Sit with that a little bit.
worst_example.note: CONFIRMED — Sophie Cuzzy remains Booked Call. BAMFAM cave without isolation = no close. This is now a confirmed outcome linked to OH=2 handling. The $111 de-risk frame would have given Sophie an easy yes. Not used.

best_example.rep_response: Proactively identified and offered NDIS pathway
best_example.note: CONFIRMED: Leanne Black = Deposit Paid. Pre-handling the logistics objection before it became a blocker = close. Know your lead's profile before the call.

best_example.parent_quote: I'm absolutely exhausted. We've tried everything.
best_example.rep_response: Acknowledged with empathy, did not push the close.
best_example.note: Sometimes the right call is to preserve the relationship. Outcome: Follow Up — consistent with reading.

weekly_top_3.note: Apr 10: Jen Howgate (Bailey) — Mark price shock. $111 not attempted. 14 total price objections across team.

Apr 7: Kirian Andreou — George was at the psych with Anthony (invested parent). Tom accepted 'I'll run past him' without isolating. Three partner deferrals from Tom now: Elisabeth Poole (dad on call), Kristy Richards (Easter delay), Kirian Andreou (George at psych). Zero isolations attempted on any.
STRENGTH — Tom's dentist metaphor deployed on Kirian Andreou naturally. Connected to Anthony's positive psych engagement as proof. Team strength confirmed.

new_patterns.pattern: Post-close activation risk from emotional under-anchoring
new_patterns.description: Jasmine Lund (Bailey, Mar 21) — Lucky close (score 3.7), confirmed 'Call Objection' in HubSpot. Consistent with no emotional investment during the call.
new_patterns.recommendation: Lucky Close Protocol: any call flagged Lucky Close triggers a Brodie activation call within 48hrs. Protocol was NOT followed for Jasmine Lund. Add to Brodie's checklist.

new_patterns.pattern: Payment plan ignorance — Rye calculated fortnightly payments live on call
new_patterns.description: Bianca JESS: Rye said 'off the top of my head, about $450, I think, a fortnight'. Price card must be memorised by all reps.
new_patterns.recommendation: Price card drill: $2,700 lump / $450 fortnightly / $225 weekly / $900 monthly. No rep should ever calculate live.

new_patterns.pattern: 'I'll find a way' — buying signal after price shock misidentified as a stall
new_patterns.description: Bianca JESS (Rye): After price shock, Bianca said 'I'll find a way.' Rye booked a follow-up. THIS IS A YES.
new_patterns.recommendation: Add 'I'll find a way' to buying signal recognition drill. Respond: 'You just said you'll find a way — I believe you. If I can find a payment schedule that works, are we doing this today?'

new_patterns.description: Score 6.8, predicted Near Close. Day 6 (Mar 29): still Booked Call in HubSpot, no DC outcome. Uncycled abandonment bomb (Tyson: 'if he was so worried about me, why would he leave me?') left anchor incomplete.
new_patterns.recommendation: Rye to call Jo Kelly Mar 30. Re-anchor only — 'I've been thinking about Tyson since our call.' Day 7 is the close of the window.

new_patterns.pattern: CONFIRMED: Price confidence correlates with close rate this week (3/3)
new_patterns.description: Week of Mar 23-29: Every confirmed close (Judith Lacey, Leanne Black, Bec Richardson) had confident single-price delivery. Every non-close had hesitant, absent, or caved price delivery. This is 6 calls with a clean split. The data is clear.
new_patterns.recommendation: Price card is now ranked as the single highest-leverage skill to train this week — above even emotional labelling. Without confident price delivery, deepening quality is irrelevant. Memorise the card.

new_patterns.pattern: Lucky Close Protocol gap — Jasmine Lund Call Objection was preventable
new_patterns.description: Jasmine Lund paid Mar 21, flagged Lucky Close Mar 21, Call Objection confirmed Mar 28-29. Brodie re-anchor call should have been placed by Mar 23. It was not. The reversal was preventable with the protocol.
new_patterns.recommendation: Harry to confirm with Brodie: Lucky Close flag triggers a 48hr activation call. Add to Brodie's standard onboarding checklist. Bailey: no new coaching sessions until transcript submitted.

new_patterns.pattern: Both-parents close miss — Tom had Dad on the call but didn't use him
new_patterns.description: Elisabeth Poole: Elizabeth cited "need to chat with Jason" as reason to defer. Jason had been on the call for the entire conversation but hadn't spoken in 20+ minutes. Tom never directed a question at Jason. One direct question to dad — "Jason, does this feel like what Michael needs?" — would have removed the blocker and likely closed. This is a new failure mode: the PASSIVE PARTNER. Different from the absent partner objection (handled well by Anthony on Judith). Needs dedicated handling.
new_patterns.recommendation: Both-parents close sequence: When both parents are live and mum defers to dad — turn immediately to dad. Don't let it be an "I'll check with him later." "Jason, you've heard everything — from your perspective, does this feel like what Michael needs right now?"

new_patterns.pattern: Analytical parent needs opportunity-cost deepening, not fear questions
new_patterns.description: Lorissa Garcia (organisational psychologist) processed all fear questions cognitively. Standard fear bomb ('what worries you most?') surfaced a risk narrative, not an emotional revelation. Tom got to surface-level fear but didn't unlock real depth. Opportunity-cost framing ('what would you regret most if you waited 6 months?') is the alternative strategy for high-functioning parents.
new_patterns.recommendation: Add analytical parent deepening sequence to Tom's drill set. Trigger: when parent is a professional in helping/coaching/corporate field — pivot from fear to opportunity-cost framing.

new_patterns.pattern: Lighthouse program introduced too early as financial escape hatch
new_patterns.description: Sky Schramm: Tom introduced the Lighthouse program before properly isolating the financial objection. This gave Sky an easy exit ('I'll look at the info and email Thursday') instead of continuing toward a $111 close. Lighthouse is a valid fallback but should only surface AFTER: isolate → $111 de-risk → weekly plan → still blocked.
new_patterns.recommendation: Lighthouse = last resort only. Sequence: isolate → $111 → weekly/fortnightly → if still no → Lighthouse. Never introduce Lighthouse unprompted when weekly payment plan hasn't been fully explored.

new_patterns.description: Elisabeth Poole: dad (Jason) was on the call the entire time but silent. After Tom completed the pitch, Elizabeth said 'Let me chat to Jason and myself.' Tom accepted this without turning to Jason. The close was available — Jason was right there. Outcome hardened to Call Objection/Price Objection in HubSpot. This is DIFFERENT from the absent partner objection: the partner is present, not absent. The fix is to address the passive partner BEFORE the mum can defer to him.
new_patterns.recommendation: Both-Parents Close sequence: When both parents are live → turn to dad directly after mum speaks. 'Jason/[Dad], you've heard everything — from your perspective, does this sound like what [son] needs?' Get his YES on record before she can defer. This neutralises the deferral before it forms.

new_patterns.pattern: Name carryover error — rep called son by wrong name mid-pitch
new_patterns.description: Tom called Riley 'Samuel' during the pitch (Kristy Richards call). Samuel was Lorissa Garcia's son — the call scored the day before. This is a context carryover error from back-to-back DCs. If the parent caught it, trust broke mid-pitch. Even if unnoticed, it signals lack of prep. This is a low-frequency but high-impact error that can cost an otherwise clean call.
new_patterns.recommendation: Prep ritual before every DC: write [Son Name] + [Mum Name] on paper. Say son's name in the first 2 minutes to anchor it. Never score > 1 call per day without this ritual. Add to Tom's weekly training doc.

new_patterns.pattern: Past-failure question never asked — gold standard positioning missed
new_patterns.description: Kristy Richards described: 'we fought for years just to get something in place... it lasted like six months.' This is the exact setup for Harry's gold standard question: 'What do you think missed the mark with previous supports?' Tom moved past it. That question does three things: surfaces the gap in prior support, lets the mum articulate what she needs, positions B&G as the answer to HER specific problem. Not using it leaves B&G as one of many options instead of THE answer.
new_patterns.recommendation: Add 'past failure' trigger to all reps: when a parent mentions previous support that didn't last or didn't work — STOP. Ask: 'What do you think missed the mark with those supports?' This is Harry's gold standard. Anthony has never asked it. Tom missed it here. It belongs in every call where past support is mentioned.

new_patterns.pattern: Easter break + unresolved partner objection = Call Objection hardening
new_patterns.description: Kristy Richards (Tom, Apr 1): Tom got a conditional yes from Kristy but didn't book a specific time with James before hanging up. Easter break (Apr 2-7) means no rep-initiated follow-up until Tuesday. Objection hardened to Call Objection in HubSpot by Apr 2. This is a sequencing/timing lesson: when the decision needs a second party's input, the re-engagement must be locked in during the same call — not left to chance. Holidays amplify the cold-lead risk from any unresolved partner objection.
new_patterns.recommendation: When partner buy-in is needed and holiday/busy period incoming: 'I know things get busy over Easter — let's lock in a 15-minute call on [specific day] with James. What's a good time?' Book it before hanging up. Never leave it as 'I'll leave that with you.'

new_patterns.pattern: Rebecca Drury Lucky Close — price objection accepted without full isolation
new_patterns.description: Rebecca Drury (Apr 1): Rep self-noted 'believed her but really should of isolated even tho it was a fkn random one, but didnt even give myself the chance to.' Deposit Paid despite partial isolation. Price Objection listed in HubSpot discovery_call_outcome. This is a Lucky Close — emotional anchor uncertain, Brodie re-activation call required within 48hrs of April 5 (back from Easter).
new_patterns.recommendation: Lucky Close Protocol: Brodie to call Rebecca Drury by April 7. Re-anchor: 'What made you decide to go ahead?' Confirm she's clear on what she signed up for. If shaky → rebook expectations conversation. Do NOT wait for W1 to find out she's not activated.

new_patterns.pattern: Single YES close signal not recognised — 'I'm in sales, I'm keen' is a $111 signal
new_patterns.description: Emily Brady (Tom, Mar 31): Emily said 'Don't worry about doing a closing pitch. I'm in sales. I'm keen to do it. I'll make it work.' Tom had already isolated price as the only blocker and got a YES on value. Instead of asking 'The $111 is literally $111 — is that doable today?', Tom booked Tuesday. This is a follow-up risk that could have been closed on-call. Pattern: rep recognises the stall but doesn't recognise the 'I'm keen' signal as a green light to attempt the small YES before booking follow-up.
new_patterns.recommendation: Small YES Close drill: when the parent has isolated to cash flow only + confirmed value + says 'I'm keen' — ask specifically for $111 before booking follow-up. '$111 is literally $111, not the big number. Is that doable today?' One question. If still no, book the follow-up. Add to weekly training doc.

new_patterns.pattern: Invested partner at appointment = unused urgency anchor
new_patterns.description: Kirian Andreou: George was at the psychologist with Anthony during the call. This means George is already investing time and money in Anthony's wellbeing. Tom never used this: 'George is literally investing in Anthony right now — the $111 just adds a mentor alongside what the psych is doing.' The partner isn't a blocker — they're already bought in. Different from Elisabeth Poole (passive partner) and Kristy Richards (absent partner). This is ACTIVE PARTNER AT APPOINTMENT.
new_patterns.recommendation: When a partner is currently at an appointment or actively supporting the child: reframe from 'need to check with partner' to 'partner is already invested — this is an AND, not an OR.' The $111 close is framed as adding to what they're already doing.

new_patterns.pattern: Info pack offer = escape route creation
new_patterns.description: Kirian Andreou: Tom asked 'Is there any more information available?' and offered to send an info pack. This gave Kirian an easy exit: 'Yeah, if you could send that through.' Every time an info pack is offered before the $111 close attempt, the parent takes the escape route. The info pack should only be offered AFTER: isolation → $111 attempt → if still no → info pack as follow-up tool.
new_patterns.recommendation: Info pack = LAST RESORT only. Sequence: value confirmation → $111 attempt → if no → propose specific follow-up time → THEN offer info pack as supporting material for the follow-up. Never offer info pack unprompted before the $111 close.
